narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score of 36 and undergraduate GPA of 3.20, which are right in line with what many successful students bring. However, the admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 49.4% chance that you might not pass it on your first try, tipping the scales toward rejecting your application to prioritize those with stronger signals of bar success. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical 5.5-ish average, suggesting your later academic performance raised concerns about sustaining the rigor needed for law school and the bar. This outweighed the positive nudge from your undergraduate GPA, which actually pushed the model toward seeing you as more likely to pass, thanks to its closeness to the average. Your LSAT score provided another helpful boost in the same direction, nearly matching the dataset norm and signaling good test-taking ability under pressure. Meanwhile, your Year 1 decile of 3 had a moderate drag, hinting at early inconsistencies that the model noted, though not as heavily as your later grades. On a brighter note, your upper-middle family income background and full-time student status both gently favored a passing prediction, aligning with patterns among those who succeed. Compared to typical students, your academics started okay but dipped notably by Year 3, unlike many who climb steadily into higher deciles. While this decision stings, especially given your unique journey as a Black woman breaking barriers, it's not the end—consider retaking courses to boost your deciles, prepping intensely for the LSAT if possible, or targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your full story. You have the foundation; building on these areas could flip the script for future applications. We're rooting for you.
